explanation: Butylene glycol is a short-chain diol used in skincare as a solvent, humectant, and penetration enhancer, helping to draw moisture to the skin surface and improve the absorption of other ingredients. It is well-tolerated by most skin types and is considered a gentler alternative to propylene glycol, with a lower sensitization profile.

Butyloctyl salicylate is a lightweight synthetic ester emollient derived from salicylic acid and fatty alcohols, used to provide a smooth, non-greasy skin feel and to help dissolve oil-based UV filters. It has no meaningful exfoliating activity — unlike salicylic acid — and is considered well-tolerated and non-comedogenic at typical formula concentrations.

Butylparaben is a longer-chain paraben preservative that has been flagged for potential weak estrogenic activity in laboratory studies and is a known contact sensitizer in individuals with paraben sensitivity. While regulatory bodies generally consider it safe at approved concentrations, it is among the parabens most often avoided by reactive skin users and those choosing to minimize endocrine-disrupting ingredient exposure.

Butylphenyl methylpropional (Lilial) is a synthetic lily-of-the-valley fragrance compound classified as one of 26 known EU fragrance allergens and subsequently banned from EU cosmetics in 2022 due to reproductive toxicity concerns. It can trigger allergic contact dermatitis on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin and its presence in a formula indicates either a product made before the ban or sold outside EU jurisdiction.

Butyrospermum parkii (shea) butter is a rich plant butter from the African shea tree, high in oleic and stearic fatty acids along with triterpene alcohols that provide emollient, occlusive, and anti-inflammatory benefits. It is well-tolerated by most skin types, though its moderate-to-high occlusive nature may not suit acne-prone or very oily skin at high concentrations.

Butyrospermum parkii butter is an alternate INCI listing for shea butter — a rich plant butter from the African shea tree high in oleic and stearic fatty acids with emollient, occlusive, and anti-inflammatory properties. It is well-tolerated by most skin types, though its occlusive nature may not suit acne-prone or very oily skin at high concentrations.

Butyrospermum parkii butter is shea butter — a rich emollient from the African shea tree high in oleic and stearic fatty acids with occlusive and anti-inflammatory properties. It is well-tolerated by most skin types, though its occlusive richness may not suit acne-prone or very oily skin at high concentrations.

C10-30 alkyl acrylate crosspolymer is a crosslinked synthetic polymer thickener closely related to carbomer, used to create gel-like textures in serums, sunscreens, and moisturizers. It is non-sensitizing, non-comedogenic, and well-tolerated across all skin types including reactive and sensitive.

C12-14 pareth-12 is a PEG-modified medium-chain alcohol surfactant and emulsifier used to stabilize oil-in-water emulsions and improve formula texture. It is generally well-tolerated at typical formula concentrations, though individuals with PEG sensitivity should note the polyethylene glycol modification.

C12-15 alkyl benzoate is a lightweight, non-greasy synthetic ester emollient widely used in sunscreens and moisturizers for its ability to dissolve oil-soluble UV filters and provide a dry, silky skin feel. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and suitable for oily and combination skin types that find heavier emollients too occlusive.

C12-16 alcohols is a blend of medium-chain fatty alcohols (lauryl through cetyl) derived from plant or synthetic sources, used as emollients and thickeners that give creams and lotions a smooth texture. Like other fatty alcohols, they are well-tolerated and non-irritating — they do not strip the skin barrier the way short-chain alcohols do.

C12-20 alkyl glucoside is a medium-to-long chain sugar-based surfactant derived from glucose and fatty alcohols, used in gentle cleansers and emulsifiers for effective yet skin-friendly cleansing. It is well-tolerated by sensitive skin and is considered a low-irritation alternative to sulfate-based surfactants.

C12-22 alkyl acrylate/hydroxyethylacrylate copolymer is a synthetic associative thickener used to build viscosity and stabilize emulsions in skincare formulas, producing a smooth, lightweight texture. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing across skin types.

C13-15 alkane is a blend of synthetic linear hydrocarbons used as a lightweight emollient and carrier similar to squalane, providing a dry, non-greasy skin feel with minimal occlusive potential. It is well-tolerated, non-comedogenic, and biodegradable, making it a popular alternative to mineral oil in modern clean formulas.

C14-22 alcohols is a blend of longer-chain fatty alcohols (myristyl through behenyl) derived from plant or synthetic sources, used as emollients, thickeners, and co-emulsifiers that contribute a smooth, rich texture. Like all fatty alcohols, they are well-tolerated and support barrier integrity without the drying or irritating effects of short-chain alcohols.

Caffeine is a methylxanthine that inhibits phosphodiesterase enzymes, promoting fat breakdown in subcutaneous tissue and reducing fluid accumulation around the eyes, making it an effective ingredient for de-puffing. It also provides antioxidant protection and UV-sensitization defense, and is well-tolerated by all skin types with no irritation risk at typical formula concentrations.

Calamine is a mixture of zinc oxide and ferric oxide with a long history of use as a soothing topical for itch, redness, and minor skin irritation. It is exceptionally well-tolerated — often used as a first-line relief ingredient for reactive, sunburned, and eczema-prone skin — and has mild astringent and antimicrobial properties from its zinc content.

Calcite (calcium carbonate) is a naturally occurring mineral used in skincare as a gentle abrasive in physical exfoliants, a white pigment in cosmetics, and an opacifying thickener. It is inert and well-tolerated, though sharp-particle grades in physical scrubs can cause microtears on reactive or compromised skin.

Calcium gluconate is the calcium salt of gluconic acid, used in skincare as a gentle soothing agent that provides ionic calcium to support barrier function and cellular signaling in skin. It is well-tolerated, non-irritating, and used in calming formulas to reduce inflammation and support compromised skin recovery.

Calcium sodium borosilicate is a synthetic glass mineral used in cosmetics as a light-diffusing texture agent and pearlescent pigment, creating a soft-glow or shimmer effect on the skin surface. The smooth, rounded particles are inert, well-tolerated, and non-comedogenic.

Calendula officinalis (marigold) flower extract is a botanicla with well-documented anti-inflammatory, wound-healing, and antioxidant properties from its flavonoid and triterpene content. It is exceptionally well-tolerated — one of the most widely used extracts for reactive, compromised, and sensitive skin — though individuals with ragweed or Asteraceae family allergies should patch-test, as cross-reactivity is possible.

Camellia japonica (Japanese camellia) flower extract provides polyphenol antioxidants and tannins with free-radical-scavenging and mild astringent properties. It is well-tolerated and used in brightening and protective formulas for its antioxidant and skin-conditioning benefits.

Camellia japonica (Japanese camellia) seed oil is a traditional Japanese beauty oil rich in oleic acid (~85%) and polyphenols with excellent emollient and antioxidant properties. It absorbs readily, rates very low on comedogenicity scales, and has been used for centuries in Japan for its ability to nourish skin and hair without heaviness.

Camellia sinensis (green tea) leaf extract is one of the most studied botanical antioxidants, rich in EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate) and catechins with potent free-radical-scavenging, anti-inflammatory, and UV-protective properties. It is exceptionally well-tolerated by all skin types and is a reliable antioxidant backbone in protective and anti-aging formulas.

Camellia sinensis (green tea) leaf powder is dried, milled tea leaf delivering catechin antioxidants including EGCG in a solid form useful in physical exfoliants, masks, and anhydrous formulas. It is well-tolerated and provides the same free-radical-scavenging and anti-inflammatory benefits as liquid green tea extract.

Camphor is a bicyclic terpene derived from camphor laurel wood with a cooling, menthol-like sensation used to temporarily relieve itch and minor discomfort. While the cooling effect can feel soothing, camphor is a known irritant at concentrations above 3% and can trigger contact dermatitis and worsen an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or sensitized skin — it is best approached with caution by those with barrier-compromised or highly reactive skin.

Canadian colloidal clay is a natural fine-particulate clay from Canadian mineral deposits, used in masks and cleansers for its oil-absorbing and pore-cleansing properties. It is well-tolerated in rinse-off formulas across most skin types, though frequent or prolonged use can over-strip barrier lipids from reactive or dry skin.

Cananga odorata (ylang ylang) flower oil is a tropical essential oil widely used for its intense floral scent, but is a well-documented contact sensitizer containing multiple fragrance allergen compounds including benzyl benzoate, benzyl salicylate, eugenol, farnesol, and geraniol. It frequently triggers allergic contact dermatitis and an itch-and-scratch cycle on reactive or fragrance-sensitive skin, particularly in leave-on products.

Candida bombicola/glucose/methyl rapeseedate ferment is a biosurfactant (sophorolipid) produced by yeast fermentation of glucose and rapeseed oil, used in skincare for its gentle cleansing, antimicrobial, and skin-conditioning properties. It is well-tolerated, biodegradable, and considered a sustainable alternative to petroleum-derived surfactants.

Caprylhydroxamic acid is a mild chelating preservative booster derived from coconut oil-sourced caprylic acid, used to enhance the efficacy of other preservatives and help prevent microbial contamination. It is considered well-tolerated and non-sensitizing, and is commonly paired with glycerin and 1,2-hexanediol in preservative-sensitive or natural-positioned formulas.
